text: The robotic hiatal hernia repair begins with the dissection of the gastrohepatic ligament up to the right cruce, which is dissected and skeletonized to expose the esophagus. The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to transect the short gastric arteries up to the left cruce. The attachments of the stomach to the left cruce are also dissected, freeing the diaphragmatic hiatus.

The surgeon performs mediastinal dissection of the esophagus using electrocautery and blunt dissection, with a Penrose drain passed around the esophagus to aid in the process. The decussation of the crura is cleared of attachments, and the posterior esophagus is mobilized using a harmonic scalpel and blunt dissection. The aorta is visible at the bottom of the screen as the esophagus is freed from its posterior attachments.

The surgeon carefully dissects fat tissue on the esophagus, avoiding injury to the anterior vagus nerve. The robotic system facilitates high mediastinal dissection and mobilization of the esophagus, reaching the level of the inferior pulmonary veins. The left-sided and right-sided inferior pulmonary veins are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon re-approximates the diaphragmatic hiatus using O-ethabon sutures, completing the hiatal hernia repair portion of the operation.

The surgeon performs a Toupet fundoplication by positioning the stomach posterior to the esophagus, utilizing the shoeshine maneuver. The stomach is then secured to the diaphragmatic hiatus at both the left and right crura. Two sutures are placed on either side, connecting the stomach to the esophagus, with the anterior vagus nerve visible between the fundoplication.
